aliases: Sweet and Sour Pork Ribs Singaporean Style

english: Sweet and Sour Pork Ribs Singaporean Style

region_of_origin: China (Guangdong); popularised in Singapore and Malaysia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Cantonese-style glazed deep-fried pork rib king; closest match is the general pork rib food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers cooked pork ribs generically; no specific code exists for the Singaporean Zi Char glazed preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Pork Rib King as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Pork Rib King at a Singaporean Zi Char stall is approximately 200 g, comprising 4–6 pieces of battered deep-fried pork ribs coated in sweet-tangy glaze, often accompanied by sliced cucumber.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — Zi Char Pork Dishes; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for Pork, fresh, loin, whole, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised (FDC ID 168293) and deep-fried battered pork analogues
nutrient_source.organization: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Pork Rib King as a complete prepared dish. Values are adapted from Singapore HPB nutritional data for Zi Char pork dishes and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked pork loin and deep-fried battered pork analogues. Saturated fat estimated from USDA pork loin cooked data (~4.8 g/100 g for the meat component, adjusted for batter and glaze contribution). Cholesterol estimated from USDA pork loin cooked data (~72 mg/100 g adjusted). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat set to -1 as disaggregated fatty acid data for this composite dish are not available in either source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Composite Foods — analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried pork with sweet glaze; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pork Rib King. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on the dish's carbohydrate composition: the batter contributes refined starch (GI ~65–70) and the sweet glaze contributes sucrose and plum sauce sugars (GI ~60–65). The blended GI is moderated by the significant protein (14.5 g) and fat (13.8 g) content which slow gastric emptying. Estimate anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database entries for battered fried foods and sweet-glazed meat dishes. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 32.4) / 100 = 17.8 per 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg/100 g; 840 mg per 200 g serving) from Worcestershire sauce, tomato sauce, and plum sauce, representing approximately 42% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg per serving.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Pork Rib King to occasional consumption, request reduced-sauce portions, and avoid consuming the residual glaze to minimise sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (9.5 g/100 g; 19 g per serving) from the sweet glaze combining sugar, plum sauce, and tomato sauce, combined with a low GI of 55 and a glycemic load of 17.8 per serving, which can elevate postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Pork Rib King sparingly, limit portion size to 100 g or less, request a less sweet glaze preparation, and pair with low-GI vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combination of high sodium (420 mg/100 g), saturated fat (~4.8 g/100 g from pork and deep-frying oil), and deep-frying preparation increases cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation and atherogenic lipid contribution.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat Pork Rib King as an occasional indulgence, keep portions to 100 g or less, and prefer steamed or stir-fried pork alternatives on a regular basis to reduce saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying and high sugar intake are associated with pro-inflammatory dietary patterns that may exacerbate joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit deep-fried and high-sugar preparations such as Pork Rib King and consider steamed or stir-fried pork alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Rib King, known in Mandarin as Páigǔ wáng (排骨王) and in Cantonese as Paai4 gwat1 wong4, is a quintessential Cantonese-style dish that has become a staple of Singaporean and Malaysian Zi Char (煮炒) eateries. The dish features boneless pork loin slices or pork ribs that are tenderised, lightly coated in a seasoned batter, and deep-fried until golden and crisp. The fried pork pieces are then tossed in a vibrant, viscous red glaze that balances sweet, tangy, and savoury notes, typically made from tomato sauce, plum sauce, Worcestershire sauce, and sugar. The result is a dish with a crispy exterior, tender and juicy interior, and an addictive sticky glaze. It is commonly served over sliced cucumbers or garnished with sesame seeds. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 245 kcal, 14.5 g protein, 13.8 g fat (including ~4.8 g saturated fat), and 16.2 g carbohydrate with 9.5 g sugar. Sodium is notably high at 420 mg/100 g. The dish has a low glycemic index of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of 17.8 per standard 200 g serving. Clinically, it is a high-concern food for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium, sugar, and saturated fat content. It is not suitable for halal or kosher diets due to pork content. Best enjoyed as an occasional treat at family-style Zi Char meals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Pork Rib King?
Can diabetics eat Pork Rib King?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Pork Rib King as a complete prepared dish. Macronutrient values are adapted from Singapore HPB Zi Char pork dish data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked pork loin and deep-fried battered pork analogues. Saturated fat and cholesterol are estimated from USDA pork loin cooked data adjusted for preparation method. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database entries for battered fried foods with sweet glazes. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite nature of the dish and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: disaggregated fatty acid and moisture data for this composite deep-fried glazed pork dish are not available in the Singapore HPB database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: pork loin and deep-fried battered pork dishes contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed as effectively zero per USDA FoodData Central cooked pork loin data (FDC ID 168293). 3. Saturated fat (4.8 g/100 g) and cholesterol (72 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked pork loin data adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption and glaze contribution; these are approximate values. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Pork Rib King exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The input portions array contained a duplicate entry (two identical 200 g serving entries); deduplicated to a single 200 g default serving. 6. The input gi_level was stated as 'low' with a GI value of 55; GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low range (≤55) and is correctly classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Halal status is false as the dish contains pork.
